revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23991619004|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:飯田町燈籠山祭り]]
[[File:飯田町燈籠山祭り3.jpg|thumb|300x300px|'''Lantern Mountain and Fireworks''': The highlight of the festival is the massive Lantern Mountain float adorned with figurines.]] [[File:飯田町燈籠山祭り4.jpg|thumb|Iida Lantern Mountain Festival]] [[File:飯田町燈籠山祭り（いいだまちとろやままつり）.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Iida Lantern Mountain Festival]] '''Iida Lantern Mountain Festival''' (飯田町燈籠山祭り, Iidamachi Toroyama Matsuri) is an annual {{ill|Dashi|en|Dashi|qid=Q851746|lt=Dashi|1=Dashi}} (festival float) [[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]] held on {{ill|July 20|en|July 20|qid=Q2728|lt=July 20|1=July 20}} and {{ill|July 21|en|July 21|qid=Q2727|lt=July 21|1=July 21}} in [[Iida Town (Suzu City)|Iida Town]], {{ill|Suzu,_Ishikawa|en|Suzu,_Ishikawa|qid=Q846345|lt=Suzu City|1=Suzu, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|qid=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}. Officially known as "Osuzumi Matsuri," it is a ritual of [[Kasuga Shrine (Suzu City, Ishikawa)|Kasuga Shrine]] located in the same town.
== Festival Overview and History of Lantern Mountain == This festival, a ritual of Kasuga Shrine in Iida Town, boasts a history of over 370 years, dating back to the early [[:en:Kan'ei era|Kan'ei era]] (1624–1645). It is designated as an [[:en:Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] of Suzu City.<ref>[http://www.city.suzu.ishikawa.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai_list.html Suzu City Cultural Properties List] (Accessed December 5, 2018)</ref>
On [[December 4, 1997]], the Kiriko festivals of the entire {{ill|Noto Peninsula|en|Noto Peninsula|qid=Q691151|lt=Noto Peninsula|1=Noto Peninsula}} were selected as one of Japan's [[Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation]].<ref>[http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/161401 Cultural Heritage Online (Agency for Cultural Affairs)] (Accessed December 5, 2018)</ref> Additionally, on [[April 24, 2015]], the festival was included as part of the Japanese Heritage designation "Illuminated Peninsula Noto ~The Enthralling Kiriko Festivals~."<ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japanese Heritage Designations (Agency for Cultural Affairs)] (Accessed December 5, 2018)</ref> There are various theories about the origins of the lantern figurines. Some suggest they were influenced by {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}'s {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|qid=Q862407|lt=Standing Nebuta|1=Aomori Nebuta Matsuri}}, a mix of different festival cultures, or an original design that evolved into the "Iida-style" Lantern Mountain. It is said that the first figurine float, approximately 7-8 ken (about 14-15 meters) high, was created in {{ill|1814|en|1814|qid=Q6943|lt=1814|1=1814}} during the [[Bunka era]] (1804–1818), thanks to the efforts of local residents.<ref name="official">Official site</ref>
Historically, there were seven Lantern Mountains, each about 10-12 shaku (3-4 meters) tall, across all neighborhoods. However, the installation of [[:en:power lines|power lines]] during the [[:en:Taisho era|Taisho era]] rendered the procession of the Lantern Mountains impossible, and the floats became smaller, with only figurines crafted from {{ill|bamboo art|ja|竹細工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo art|qq=}}. By the early [[:en:Showa era|Showa era]], the focus shifted to children's hand dances, and Lantern Mountains gradually faded into obscurity. After being featured only during special commemorations or grand festivals, they began to reappear at this festival by the late Showa period. By the early {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|qid=Q234510|lt=Heisei era|1=Heisei era}}, eight neighborhood floats and a single Lantern Mountain, standing approximately 16 meters tall and weighing about 5 tons, were created every other year for the festival.<ref name="official" />
Lantern Mountains are layered from the bottom upwards with a base (jiyama), a platform (yatai), frame partitions (wakushōji), a small pedestal (kodai), a large pedestal (ōdai), and figurines. Many high-hanging lanterns are also attached.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Iida Lantern Mountain Festival - Japanese Heritage "Illuminated Peninsula Noto ~The Enthralling Kiriko Festivals~"|url=https://notokiriko.ishikawa.jp/kiriko/jp/kiriko/iida.php|website=notokiriko.ishikawa.jp|access-date=July 15, 2023}}</ref>
Recent efforts aim to restore the festival to its original form. In {{ill|2011|en|2011|qid=Q1994|lt=2011|1=2011}}, a second Lantern Mountain was produced, followed by a third in {{ill|2012|en|2012|qid=Q1990|lt=2012|1=2012}}. Ultimately, the goal is to return all eight floats to Lantern Mountains, reviving the festival's historical grandeur through a unified community effort.
== Scenes During the Festival == The instruments used during the procession include drums, bells, and flutes mounted on the floats. The pullers synchronize their movements with chants of "yassā yassā" or "sā yassā," pulling the floats together.
When stationary, "Gion-bayashi" music is played, to which the pullers respond with the chant "yōiyoi-yoi." The Gion-bayashi has three variations, each progressively shorter, starting from the longest.
At the start of the procession, the leader's flute signals the pulling team's commencement. A song called "Kyārage" is then performed to set the rhythm for pulling the float.
=== Kyārage === Kyārage is a traditional chant sung to initiate the movement of stationary floats or adjust the pace of the procession. The chant also coordinates distance between floats or reignites the team's momentum during a slowdown. It begins with "sōra-e," followed by verses unique to the singer or neighborhood.
==== Example of Kyārage ==== One popular version: * "Ebisu Daikoku, guardians of Iida's town, protecting its east and west."
== Key Dates == The festival spans two days, with a pre-festival on July 19 and a fireworks display on the evening of July 20. The main events occur as follows: * '''July 19''' - Pre-festival and Evening Float Procession * '''July 20''' - Osuzumi Festival (Joint Procession) *** Neighborhood rounds (morning) *** Assembly at Kasuga Shrine (afternoon) *** Procession and fireworks near Azumabashi Bridge (evening) * '''July 21''' - Post-Festival (Joint Procession) *** Assembly of all floats (noon) *** Procession across all neighborhoods (all day) *** Closing ceremony (late night)
== Cultural Designation == * '''July 10, 1996''' - Designated an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Suzu City.
== Participation == Some neighborhoods accept participants as float pullers. The Lantern Mountain procession on July 20 is open to all without prior registration. Happi coats are available for rent via the Iida Town Festival Committee.
== See Also == * [[Noto Kiriko Festivals]] * [[Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation]] * {{ill|Japan Heritage|en|Japan Heritage|qid=Q18382798|lt=Japan Heritage|1=Japan Heritage}}
== External Links == * [http://toroyama.com/ Official Iida Lantern Mountain Festival Website] * {{Wayback|url=http://homepage1.nifty.com/ueno/|title=Garakuya|date=20000303101836}} (Introduction to Hokuriku Festivals) * [https://www.travel.co.jp/guide/article/4564/ Travel.jp Travel Guide]
{{Japanese Cultural Properties}}
* {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gifu Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|Gujo Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gujo Odori|qq=}} * {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|Owari Tsushima Tennō Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari Tsushima Tennō Festival|qq=}} – One of the {{ill|Three Great River Festivals of Japan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great River Festivals of Japan|qq=}}. * {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Matsuri|qq=}} – One of the {{ill|Three Great Festivals of Japan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Festivals of Japan|qq=}}. * {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|Tenjin Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjin Matsuri|qq=}} – Part of the Three Great Festivals of Japan and the {{ill|Three Great Summer Festivals of Osaka|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Summer Festivals of Osaka|qq=}}. * {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgo Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|Yumura Onsen (Hyōgo)|lt=Yumura Onsen (Hyōgo)|lt=Yumura Onsen Fire Festival|ja|湯村温泉 (兵庫県)#イベント|ja|湯村温泉 (兵庫県)#イベント|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yumura Onsen (Hyōgo)|1=Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8061163}} * {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|Tottori Shan-Shan Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shan-Shan Festival|qq=}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|Awa Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awa Odori|qq=}} – Its {{ill|Tokushima City Awa Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima City Awa Odori|qq=}} is one of the {{ill|Three Great Festivals of Shikoku|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Festivals of Shikoku|qq=}}. * {{ill|Kochi Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kochi Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|YOSAKOI Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/YOSAKOI Festival|qq=}} – One of the Three Great Festivals of Shikoku. * {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|Hakata Gion Yamakasa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakata Gion Yamakasa|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} ''' {{ill|Yamaga Lantern Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Lantern Festival|qq=}} * {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} ''' {{ill|Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sabantuy|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sabantuy|qq=}} – A summer festival held along the middlereaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Volga River|qq=}} in {{ill|Russia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}.
* {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Winter Matsuri|lt=Winter Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}
*
